original_abstract: Background: Global labour markets continue to undergo significant transformations resulting from socio-political instability combined with rises in structural inequality, employment insecurity, and poor working conditions. Confronted by these challenges, global institutions are providing policy guidance to protect and promote the health and well-being of workers. This article provides an account of how the International Labour Organization’s Decent Work Agenda contributes to the work policy agendas of the World Health Organization and the World Bank.
humanized_abstract: Abstract The world of work is undergoing profound change, driven by a complex array of factors including political instability, increasing income inequality, job insecurity and poor working conditions. In response, international organizations are beginning to make policy recommendations aimed at improving the health and wellbeing of workers. This article offers a first-time account of the influence of the International Labour Organization’s (ILO) Decent Work Agenda on the work policy agendas of the World Health Organization (WHO) and the World Bank.
